title: Kaiming He
excerpts: **Kaiming He** ( Chinese : 何恺明 ; pinyin : *Hé Kǎimíng* ) is a Chinese computer scientist who primarily researches computer vision and deep learning . <sup>[[ 1 ]]()</sup> He is an associate professor at Massachusetts Institute of Technology and works part-time as a Distinguished Scientist at Google DeepMind. <sup>[[ 2 ]]()</sup> He is known as one of the creators of the residual neural network (ResNet) architecture. <sup>[[ 2 ]]()</sup> <sup>[[ 3 ]]()</sup>

title: Kaiming He | MIT School of Engineering
excerpts:
Section Title: Kaiming He
Content:
Ask an Engineer
[Home](https://engineering.mit.edu)

Kaiming He
Kaiming He
Faculty
Department
[Electrical Engineering and Computer Science](https://engineering.mit.edu/department/electrical-engineering-computer-science)
Kaiming He joined the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science as an associate professor in February 2024. His research interests cover a wide range of topics in computer vision and deep learning. He is currently focused on building computer models that can learn representations and develop intelligence from and for the complex world. In the long term, he aims to augment human intelligence with improved AI. Before joining MIT, he was a research scientist at Facebook AI. He earned a BS from Tsinghua University and a PhD from the Chinese University of Hong Kong.

Title: Diffuse and Disperse: Image Generation with Representation Regularization
Authors: Runqian Wang , Kaiming He
View a PDF of the paper titled Diffuse and Disperse: Image Generation with Representation Regularization, by Runqian Wang and 1 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental)
Abstract: The development of diffusion-based generative models over the past decade has largely proceeded independently of progress in representation learning.

title: Biography:Kaiming He - HandWiki
excerpts:
Section Title: Biography : Kaiming He > Namespaces > Page actions
Content:
**Kaiming He** ( Chinese : 何恺明 ; pinyin : *Hé Kǎimíng* ) is a Chinese computer scientist who primarily researches computer vision and deep learning . <sup>[[ 1 ]]()</sup> He is an associate professor at Massachusetts Institute of Technology and works part-time as a Distinguished Scientist at Google DeepMind. <sup>[[ 2 ]]()</sup> He is known as one of the creators of residual neural network (ResNet). <sup>[[ 2 ]]()</sup> <sup>[[ 3 ]]()</sup>

title: Kaiming He — Grokipedia
excerpts:
Section Title: Kaiming He
Content:
<sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> In 2016, He joined Facebook AI Research (FAIR) as a research scientist, rising to Research Scientist Director by 2024, during which time he led innovations in self-supervised learning, including Momentum Contrast (MoCo, nominated for CVPR 2020 Best Paper Award) and Masked Autoencoders (MAE). <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[2]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[3]]()</sup>]()</sup> His most influential work, the 2016 CVPR Best Paper Award-winning *Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition* , introduced ResNets with skip connections to enable training of very deep networks, achieving top performance in ImageNet and COCO challenges and amassing over 700,000 citations across his publications as of May 2025.

title: Kaiming He - People | MIT CSAIL
excerpts:
I am best known for my work on [Deep Residual Networks (ResNets)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1512.03385) , which is the most-cited paper of the twenty-first century, according to a [*Nature* article](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-025-01125-9) .
The proposed residual connections are now being used everywhere in modern deep learning models, including Transformers (e.g., ChatGPT), AlphaGo Zero, AlphaFold, and virtually all GenAI models today, among many others. I am also known for my work on visual perception (e.g., [Faster R-CNN](https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.01497) , [Mask R-CNN](https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870) ) and self-supervised learning (e.g., [MoCo](https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.05722) , [MAE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.06377) ). My publications have over [700,000](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=DhtAFkwAAAAJ&hl=en) citations (as of May 2025).

title: Kaiming He Initialization in Neural Networks - Math Proof | Towards Data Science
excerpts:
Section Title: Kaiming He Initialization in Neural Networks – Math Proof > ... > II. Backward Pass
Content:
First, we use the chain rule and the fact that the derivative of a linear product is its linear coefficient – in this case, *Wₖ* . Second, we leverage *Assumption 2* stating that gradients and weights are independent of each other. Using independence, the variance of the product becomes the product of variances, which is equal to zero since the weights are assumed to be initialized with zero means. Hence, the expectation of the gradient of *L w.r.t. x* is zero. Third, we use the chain rule to link *Δyₖ* and *Δxₖ ₊* ₁ as the partial derivative of *x* *w.r.t. y* is ReLU’s derivative taken in *y* . Fourth, recalling the derivative of ReLU, we compute the expectation of *Δyₖ* using the previous equation. As *f'(x)* is split into two parts with an equal probability of *½* , we can write it as a sum of two terms: expectation over ℝ⁺ and ℝ⁻, respectively.
